AAASeparate (auth, authz, acct)Combined auth + authz
Per-command authzYesNo
Multi-vendorMostly CiscoAll vendors
Best forDevice admin (CLI)Network access (Wi-Fi, 802.1X, VPN)

Both on Cisco ISE: the flagship AAA platform supports both protocols simultaneously. Enterprise best practice: TACACS+ for device admin, RADIUS for network access, one ISE cluster serves both.

Scenario selection:

  • 'Control which commands each admin can run on routers' → TACACS+ (per-command authz).
  • 'Authenticate Wi-Fi users against Active Directory' → RADIUS (WPA2-Enterprise).
  • 'Log every command typed on a switch' → TACACS+ (granular accounting).
  • 'Assign VLANs to 802.1X-authenticated devices' → RADIUS (Access-Accept with VLAN attribute).
  • 'Open standard, works with Aruba and Cisco together' → RADIUS.
  • 'Full packet encryption for AAA traffic' → TACACS+.

2-3 questions guaranteed on this distinction. Memorize the table.

hack.memory: Four attribute pairs: TCP/UDP, Full/Password, Separate/Combined, Admin/Access. Say it as a chant: 'TACACS TCP Total Separate Admin, RADIUS UDP Password Combined Access.'
hack.practice: Write out the comparison table from memory. Do it three times over three days. By exam day it should take 90 seconds flat. This is the single highest-yield memorization drill for Domain 2.8.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. Exam: 2-3 guaranteed questions — ROI on memorizing this table is massive.

2.8.g.info:
Cloud-managed networking moves the management plane from on-prem hardware (like a WLC or DNA Center) to a vendor-hosted cloud dashboard reachable via browser. Devices phone home to the cloud for configuration, monitoring, and software updates. The canonical example is Cisco Meraki, where every AP, switch, firewall, and camera is managed from a single dashboard.meraki.com URL.

Architecture:

  • Data plane — stays on-device. Switches forward frames, APs bridge wireless traffic, firewalls enforce policy. Client data never goes through the cloud.
  • Management plane — lives in the cloud. Config, monitoring, alerting, updates all happen via HTTPS to the cloud dashboard.
  • Control plane — split. Most control (routing, STP, radio decisions) stays on-device; long-term policies and some RF optimization happen in the cloud.

Contrast with traditional:

  • Traditional — CLI per device, maybe a WLC for wireless and Prime/DNA Center for wired. All on-prem. Scales linearly with admin effort.
  • Cloud-managed — one dashboard for thousands of devices across hundreds of sites. Each new device self-registers when plugged in.

Cisco cloud platforms:

  • Meraki — fully cloud-native. No on-prem controller.
  • Catalyst Center (formerly DNA Center) — on-prem appliance, optional cloud integration. More enterprise-focused.
  • SD-WAN Viptela/vManage — cloud or on-prem, manages WAN edge routers.
  • Catalyst 9800-CL — cloud-hosted WLC instance (public/private cloud).

hack.memory: Cloud-managed = management plane in the cloud, data plane on-device. Meraki is the poster child. Client traffic never hits the cloud — only config and monitoring do.
hack.practice: Browse to the Meraki dashboard demo (meraki.cisco.com/sandbox or similar). See the single pane showing APs, switches, client stats. Compare to the complexity of managing the same gear via WLC + Prime on-prem.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25/29. Exam: 'what is the key characteristic of cloud-managed networking?' = management via cloud dashboard; devices phone home.

2.8.g.info:
Zero-touch provisioning (ZTP) is the cloud-managed model's superpower. Plug a new device in, give it internet access, and it auto-configures from the cloud. No console cable, no manual CLI, no local admin intervention.

ZTP flow (Meraki example):

  1. Admin adds the device's serial number to the Meraki dashboard organization (at procurement or later).
  2. Admin applies a template or network assignment to that serial.
  3. Device is shipped to a branch office, plugged into the wall by a non-technical person.
  4. Device boots, gets IP via DHCP, reaches out to dashboard.meraki.com (HTTPS).
  5. Cloud authenticates the device by serial/certificate, pushes its assigned config.
  6. Device comes online fully configured within 1-3 minutes.

Why it matters: deploying 100 branch offices traditionally requires a network engineer at each site (or shipping pre-configured gear). ZTP lets you ship stock devices to each site; a retail employee plugs them in; they come up configured. Deployment time drops from days to hours, cost drops dramatically.

Cisco IOS-XE PnP (Plug and Play): similar concept for Catalyst 9000 series, integrates with Catalyst Center. Device contacts a 'PnP Server' (on-prem or cloud) and gets its config.

Security: ZTP relies on device certificate authentication + serial number verification. Meraki certs are baked into hardware at manufacture. Rogue devices can't fake being a Meraki device.

Exam framing: 'what is zero-touch provisioning?' = device self-configures from the cloud on first boot. 'What's the benefit of cloud-managed networking?' includes ZTP as a primary answer.

hack.memory: ZTP = 'ship it, plug it, done.' Serial number + cloud template = full config without touching the CLI. Meraki's signature feature.
hack.practice: Read a Meraki ZTP case study — the 'deploy 200 stores in a month' narrative. Contrast with a traditional deployment requiring network engineer visits. This viscerally proves ZTP's value.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: 'which feature of cloud-managed networking lets devices auto-configure?' = zero-touch provisioning.

2.8.g.info:
Cloud-managed networking has two non-negotiable requirements that distinguish it from on-prem:

1. Internet connectivity to the cloud dashboard. Every device must reach the vendor's cloud endpoints over HTTPS (typically TCP 443, sometimes additional UDP ports for tunneling). If the internet connection fails, management is lost — though data plane keeps working (see 2.8.g.4).

Typical endpoints (Meraki example): *.meraki.com, *.ikarem.io, specific IPs documented by vendor. Enterprise firewalls must allow outbound to these destinations. No inbound access required — devices initiate all connections.

2. Active subscription license. Cloud-managed platforms use per-device, time-based licenses. Meraki licenses are 1, 3, 5, 7, or 10 years per device. If the license expires, management access is progressively restricted:

  • Day 0-30 after expiry — grace period, warnings only.
  • Day 30-90 — dashboard becomes read-only.
  • Day 90+ — device may stop passing traffic (depends on product line).

Other typical requirements:

  • DNS resolution (devices need to resolve dashboard hostnames).
  • NTP (for cert validation).
  • Sufficient bandwidth for config pulls and stats uploads (typically small — kilobits per device).

Enterprise policy gotchas: strict firewall rules can block cloud management. Some regulated environments (air-gapped, sensitive government) cannot use cloud-managed platforms at all — for these, Catalyst Center on-prem is the answer.

hack.memory: Two mandatory things: internet + license. Miss either and cloud management breaks. Enterprise firewalls often block vendor endpoints — allowlist before you deploy.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'what are the two requirements for cloud-managed networking?' = internet connectivity to the cloud + active subscription license. Exam pure recall.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: 'what does cloud-managed require?' = internet + license.

2.8.g.info:
What happens when the internet connection to the cloud fails? Most cloud-managed platforms are designed to keep forwarding traffic normally while management is offline — the data plane is decoupled from the cloud.

Meraki offline behavior:

  • Existing traffic: keeps working. APs keep broadcasting SSIDs. Switches keep forwarding. Firewalls keep applying rules. Clients don't notice.
  • Existing clients: stay associated, keep working.
  • New authentications (RADIUS-dependent): may fail if the RADIUS server is also cloud-hosted or the cloud is used as a proxy.
  • Configuration changes: impossible. Dashboard is unreachable.
  • Monitoring: stale. Stats and alerts don't reach the dashboard.
  • Firmware updates: deferred until cloud returns.

How it works: devices cache their last-known-good config in local flash. On boot, they try to reach the cloud; if they can't, they use the cached config and keep operating. When cloud connectivity returns, devices sync and download any config changes made during the outage.

Duration of offline tolerance: indefinite for data plane. Some features (captive portal, Meraki Auth, Air Marshal wireless IPS) degrade after extended outages. License grace periods apply (see 2.8.g.3).

Design implication: even cloud-managed networks need resilient internet. Dual WAN (primary + backup LTE) is common for branch offices running Meraki. A site losing internet loses manageability even if data still flows.

hack.memory: Cloud offline = blind but not broken. Traffic flows; management doesn't. Cache + local forwarding keep the network alive. Once cloud returns, devices sync.
hack.practice: Conceptual flashcard. Exam hook: 'what happens to a Meraki network if internet to dashboard fails?' = devices continue forwarding with cached config; no config changes until restored.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: 1 question possible on offline behavior.

2.8.g.info:
CCNA tests awareness of three management paradigms. Know the tradeoffs:

1. Traditional CLI (SSH/console per device).

  • Pros: full control, no dependencies, no licensing, expert-friendly, scriptable (Ansible, Python).
  • Cons: doesn't scale — admin effort grows linearly with device count. No single pane. Config drift common.
  • Best for: small networks, labs, power users, regulated / air-gapped environments.

2. On-prem controller (WLC, Catalyst Center, Prime).

  • Pros: centralized management, GUI, still on-site (data sovereignty), scales better than CLI.
  • Cons: on-prem infrastructure to maintain, upgrade, secure. Controller failure is a single point of failure.
  • Best for: medium-large enterprises with network engineers; regulated environments wanting centralization without cloud.

3. Cloud-managed (Meraki, Catalyst 9800-CL, SD-WAN vManage cloud).

  • Pros: zero-touch provisioning, single pane across all sites, automatic updates, no on-prem controller. Great for distributed orgs.
  • Cons: internet-dependent for management, subscription cost over time, data leaves your network for management purposes.
  • Best for: retail chains, distributed orgs, MSPs, companies prioritizing operational simplicity.

Real world: hybrid is common. HQ data center = traditional CLI or Catalyst Center. Branch offices = Meraki cloud-managed. Wi-Fi everywhere = cloud-managed WLAN with local breakout. One size does not fit all.

hack.memory: Three tiers: CLI → on-prem controller → cloud. More automation each step, more dependencies each step. Pick per situation; most enterprises mix.
hack.practice: Flashcard the three tiers with one pro and one con each. Exam scenario: 'retailer with 200 stores, no on-site IT' → cloud-managed (ZTP is the killer feature). 'Air-gapped lab' → CLI.
